speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Sonnenschein, I would like to ask you if you will tell us, please, how did you meet your husband in ghetto? And a little bit about your wedding, and how long you lived as a husband and wife, and something about that part of your life.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd be glad to. My husband was from Czechoslovakia. And he came with a transport. This transport was also the intelligentsia. All the lawyers, doctors, engineers, all academia was deported. He was an engineer. I met him through my work. And since I was still alive inside-- hungry, still alive, still having hope-- we were attracted to each other. But it wasn't like in normal times. We could never go out together. We could never do the things young people like to do-- to a concert, to a movie, sitting on a park bench. We could never do any of those things. The only thing to be able to be together was really only to get married. Rumkowski has been being given the authority to perform a wedding ceremony. And it was recognized by the city of Lodz. Mary also had a boyfriend. He was from Poland. And so the two of us got married on the same day.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say Mary, can you tell us who Mary is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Mary was the girl I worked with in the office. Mary was the girl that went with me to Auschwitz and Stutthof. Mary was the girl that died after her mother and sister got killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Schifflingr, S-C-H-I-F-F-L-I-N-G-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where's she from?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was actually from Germany. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I think of Mary a lot. We went through so much together. We were together when they beat up Rumkowski. We went together to Auschwitz and Stutthof, and that's where she died.

speaker: interviewer
text: And both of you--

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we were married together on the same day. We each had-- Mary wanted to be in white, so she had a white blouse. And somehow she got a veil. I had a light blue blouse, and I got a veil-- little veil. Even hungry, for a tiny moment, we were happy. I didn't look bad for the simple reason that I was [? swollen. ?] So I looked like I had full cheeks, which was really not true. But looking at the picture, which I will show, you think I do. It lasted maybe-- I don't know-- two, three minutes. Not much. Rumkowski arranged to have coffee-- black [NON-ENGLISH]-- in tin cups, and what looked like a honey cake, but it really wasn't a honey cake. It looked like. But it really didn't matter. For the fleeting moment, you were happy. And you dared to hope maybe it will continue. 11 months later, he was killed in Stutthof.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say he was killed in Stutthof, that means you were transferred from that ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In August 1944-- yeah. In August, 1944, the order came, I suppose from Berlin, to liquidate the ghetto. So you see, regardless of our wonderful work that we performed for the Germans, regardless of whatever we did, we were told-- they were so good in lying to us. We were told we will be going to Germany to work in labor camps. Because Germany needs people, and we proved that we are good workers. And that's where we will be going. Now, I don't know today whether anyone believed it or not. It didn't make the slightest difference. We were told we can take a small suitcase or knapsack, whatever we had, with our personal belongings. And we were told by Hans Biebow, the German administrator, to take a fork and spoon so when we are given food, we don't have to eat with our fingers. Now, that sounded encouraging. It sounded like we'll be getting food. We were rounded up, transport after transport after transport, in cattle cars. There were about 50 of us in one cattle car. We couldn't sit-- no room to sit. We took turns sitting. It was closed. We didn't get food. We didn't get water. They put a container in that cattle car to relieve ourselves. We were men, women. In ours were still two children. There were very few. In ours was two children-- two little girls that I remembered. Every so often, the guard-- the train came to a stop, a guard opened the door, and one of us emptied the container and put it back. Now, I don't know if anyone can understand what it meant to relieve yourself in front of men, women, and children. It was the greatest indignity that we have encountered so far.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like I said before, wearing the star of David, the badge of shame, like the Nazis referred to, didn't bother me. But this bothered me, and it bothered every one of us. Where was our dignity? What did they do to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember when they said that we are sub-humans. Maybe that's how you treat sub-humans. They don't deserve to be treated like human beings. The journey lasted five days. We had not the faintest idea where we are going-- nothing. We arrived at Auschwitz-Birkenau. It's together on the station. I had never known, and nobody knew, what Auschwitz meant, because nobody had come back from Auschwitz to tell us. A guard-- before a guard opened the door, or right as he opened it, actually, we have seen men, terribly like skeletons, in that camp, in their striped uniforms and little funny caps, striped also, on their heads. It was a Jewish prisoner, inmate of Auschwitz, who opened the door and helped us out. And I hear him like I would hear him today. He said, there is gas here. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was numb by that. I was totally numb. I was hungry, humiliated, and now he said gas. And when you stepped out, you see the smoke from the chimneys. Because that's where the bodies that were gassed ended up-- in the crematoria, in the chimney. And the smoke came out day and night, day and night. And when I referred to what people said, we can take our personal belongings, we can take some cutlery. We were ordered to undress, to strip.

speaker: interviewer
text: By whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the Nazis in charge of Auschwitz. And the first thing they did was separate men from women. That was the first thing. There was no goodbye-- not to my father nor to my husband. We were still young. I was in my 20s. He was a few years older. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were only married 11 months. My father, who believed in the last moment that the time will come when there will be bread and no borders-- there was no goodbye to him. They were taken to [? other ?] field. We were thousands of women. And after we were stripped, I don't know if you have heard the name Josef Mengele. He was called the angel of death.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Josef is like Josef. Mengele is M-E-N-G-E-L-E. He used to be a doctor-- a medical doctor. He had taken the oath of Hippocrates to save the sick, to help the ones that need help. And he became-- he switched his profession. Instead of helping the sick, he had them killed. And he stood before us. We tried to cover our nakedness, you understand. But he ordered us to stand straight.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when you say that he ordered you to be naked, was that in one big room where--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was outdoor field. In the outdoor field, where everybody that passed by could see you. Prisoners--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many of you women were naked standing in a field?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Thousands. But first of all, our entire transport. It was in groups. It was an enormous amount of women. But first, it was our transport. It was always followed in groups. And Mengele approached us. Now, he wasn't a dream of Adolf Hitler-- tall, blonde, blue-eyed. He was not at tall. He was dark, dark hair, piercing eyes. He wore a beautiful white uniform. Boots that were shined by prisoners like a mirror. You could mirror [? in it. ?] And he had a whip in hand. They never went without a whip in their hand. And we were told to stand straight, arms at our sides, and he inspected our bodies, one by one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he inspecting the bodies because he wanted to see if you are ill, or because he want to humiliate you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, both. First of all, if there was the slightest scar on the body, the slightest scar-- it could have been an insignificant operation, but slightest scar-- he didn't have to say anything. He only pointed with the thumb to the left. Anyone with gray hair to the left. The few remaining children to the left. And I've seen them marching to the gas chambers. And that was the end for them. The other ones that he thought are still working material he sent to the right. And we were taken to showers. Before entering the shower stall, you know, we were numb with fear. A German woman guard approached us, and she shaved all our bodily hair from top to bottom. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We marched into this shower. When we came out, I can assure you we didn't recognize each other, all head shaved. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] We were thrown some rags. We never seen our clothes again. We sure didn't need cutlery. They threw us some rags-- just tossed it around. And for some reason, I didn't catch underwear. I don't think everybody got underwear. Some did. [? Paola ?] did. My mother did. I didn't. Until the end of the-- until I was liberated, I didn't even have underwear. I had a skirt. I got a string to pull it up. It was much too large. And a blouse. And we all got wooden shoes. Now, with that accomplished, we were marched to the bunks. They were inside. There were five to the bunk. The number five somehow appealed to them, because everything was done in fives. When we lined up for roll-call, I guess in the pre-interview, you asked whether I worked. Upon our arrival in Auschwitz, all we did was standing in roll call. While we stood in roll call, they beat us for no reason whatsoever. They enjoyed it. And they counted us, forever counted us. It was 5 times 15, 20, and in the hundreds.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say roll call, that means standing in lineups-- what we would say today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just line up, stand straight, and they counted everybody. And half of the time, they made mistakes, so they started again. Whether it was done on purpose or not, I cannot tell you. And not only that, we had to perform exercises. It was strictly to be mean, which they knew how to be, for the amusement of the guards, probably. We had to do stretching our arms for the longest time. We had to do knee bends. Now, can you image if you are so hungry, so desperate, so scared, to perform exercises-- and God help you if you didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how long you have been in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say close to a month. You see, once we were in the concentration camp-- in the ghetto we still knew the time. We had clocks. We had no idea the day, the date, the hour. We had no idea. We knew that they woke us up-- roll-call, it went all over the place-- it was dark outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you stayed there for about a month. How do you know that your husband and your father had been killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: After it was over-- let me just tell you our food ration. One slice of bread, one soup-- again, with red beets. They had that thing with the red beets. Black coffee that we had to sip five of us. That was the diet. But something else, Victoria-- we couldn't relieve ourselves. That started with Auschwitz and continued for the rest of time I was there, in all the camps. They took us in groups of five to latrines. That's a hole in the ground. And we stood in front of the latrine, waiting our turn out to relieve ourselves. Even this they didn't let us do. And you had to fight so hard not to break down. The humiliation-- you were so low, like the lowest creature. And you had to pray all the time-- God, let me keep my soul. They don't see it. I don't want to surrender to them. What they don't see they can't take away from me. And you had to put all your willpower, all your prayer to remain a human being. And you did it. After Auschwitz, we were transported to Stutthof, close to Danzig-- Gdanskz in Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just to backtrack for a second, when your father and your husband has been separated and sent out, where were they sent?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were also in Stutthof. After Auschwitz, the whole transport-- I don't know. It was, I guess, in the end 60,000. The whole transport-- not 60,000. Part of it went to Auschwitz, part of it went to Ravensbruck. But part that went to Auschwitz were sent to Stutthof as well, and from then on further on. I know he was in Stutthof together with my first husband. And I know that he was killed there, because after the war, I met-- I ran into somebody, a man that was in that camp, and he told us. He didn't tell us how he was killed, but that he was killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father, as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father and [INAUDIBLE], yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, from Auschwitz, you were transported--

speaker: interviewee
text: To Stutthof.

speaker: interviewer
text: To Stutthof, which is in which country?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's also in Poland. It's also in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened then, in short, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: In Stutthof, it was a continuation of Auschwitz. Whereas in Auschwitz we had bunks-- I told you five to a bunck-- in Stutthof, we had nothing, only the bare floor. We slept on the floor. We ate our soup on the floor, and the coffee we sipped like animals. No blanket. Well, in September it was still fairly warm. But then the snow came. The rain came and the snow came. And we still stood with our bare head. And I still had no underwear. And we stood there again for hours in roll call It was totally dark when we were called.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your work in Stutthof? They give you any jobs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing, again. It consisted of standing in the roll call, being taken to the latrine. Seldom could we wash. Waiting for the bread, then waiting for the soup.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long you had stayed there?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Stutthof we stayed three months. And I know that we left in December because we were told to line up again.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was December which year?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1944. Again, we didn't know what they have in  mind, right? We never knew. As it turned out, we were taken to a forced labor camp in Dresden, Germany. But let me point out you something, which is the reason I wanted to live. I still had my mother and my sister with me. Now, you must understand that they did not know. Had they known the relationship, you can rest assured they would have separated us-- maybe killed one in front of the other to hurt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you saying that both your mother and sister were with you in a camp, and yet nobody knew that you were related?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you keep that secret?

speaker: interviewee
text: We hardly talked. There was not much talk.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you had the same last name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but-- oh, we had no name. They took our name away. We wore numbers. Nobody had a name.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you mention the numbers, did you get one of these?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't get tattooed. Our transport-- I don't know how their minds worked. I cannot tell you. We were all shaved to make it impossible to run away or to humiliate us. I cannot tell you why did they did what they did. But nobody knew. I told you, first of all, we all looked alike-- big, shaved head. And when I talked to my mother and my sister, I talked to Mary and then other women, too. We never knew the name. I had no idea to whom I talked. I know the ones I knew from the ghetto. But nobody had a name.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were only the numbers, and that's how--

speaker: interviewee
text: Only numbers, only numbers. And they just barked out the numbers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you know what they did for our Jewish holidays once? Somebody asked me, what did you do on a Jewish holiday? I said, A, I didn't know when it was. I had no calendar. I didn't know what time was it. Maybe-- no. I had no idea.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they came into the camp, equipped with their whip.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the guards. And they said, today is Rosh Hashanah, our new year. Ten days later, they said, today-- well, I assumed it was ten days later; I cannot tell-- Yom Kippur, the day of atonement, our high holidays. And you know what they did to make us think of our holidays? They beat us senselessly. And, you know, we had to watch being beaten. I had to watch while my mother was being beaten and [? Paola, ?] my sister. And they in turn had to watch when I was beaten. And there was nothing, absolutely nothing, that anyone could have done. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was the hard part.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you-- how long you have stayed there?

speaker: interviewee
text: How long in Stutthof?

speaker: interviewer
text: Stutthof, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, OK. Now we came-- we are assembled and taken on a train to Dresden, Germany. And I know that it was December, because arriving in Dresden, Germany, there were Christmas decorations on the street. They marched us. Dresden didn't have an [? ammunition ?] factory, but they had requisitioned a house. I know the street [INAUDIBLE] 68. I remember the address where they established an ammunition factory. That's where they took us.

speaker: interviewer
text: We are coming now to the close of our tape. We are going to stop. However, we'll come back, and then we'll continue on to see what's happened when you had gone to a death march. Just in a few minutes we will do that.
